children.name: Numenius
children.description: Curlews — large, long-billed waders whose haunting, liquid calls are among the most evocative sounds in the natural world. The Eurasian curlew (storspov) is one of Sweden's most recognisable waders, its long decurved bill perfectly adapted for probing into mud, peat, and grass root zones to extract earthworms, insects, and crustaceans. The bubbling song of the curlew, delivered in soaring display flight over boggy farmland and coastal meadows, is the defining sound of the Swedish countryside in spring — but it is a sound that is becoming scarcer as agricultural intensification and drainage destroy the wet grasslands the curlew requires for nesting. Sweden holds one of Europe's most significant curlew breeding populations and its continued decline is one of the most urgent conservation concerns facing Swedish ornithology.

children.name: Numenius arquata
children.description: The storspov — Sweden's largest wader and one of its most urgently declining breeding birds. Its long, downcurved bill can reach 15 cm in females. The fluty, bubbling song — rising into a cascade of liquid notes — is the quintessential sound of Swedish coastal meadows and boggy farmland in spring. Breeding numbers have halved since the 1980s due to agricultural drainage and predator pressure. Swedish west coast meadows and the larger lake systems are key breeding strongholds.

children.name: Gallinago
children.description: Snipe — cryptically streaked marsh birds whose remarkable aerial displays produce one of the most unusual sounds in the animal kingdom. The common snipe (enkelbeckasin) is a master of camouflage, its brown-and-buff streaked plumage rendering it almost invisible among sedge and rush as it stands motionless. When flushed it explodes from underfoot in a fast, erratic, zigzagging flight that historically made it the most challenging of all quarry species for hunters — giving rise to the word 'sniper'. In spring, male snipe perform 'drumming' or 'bleating' display dives over their territories: as the bird stoops at speed, the two outermost tail feathers spread and vibrate in the airstream to produce a distinctive pulsing sound (meckrande in Swedish) audible for hundreds of metres.

children.name: Gallinago gallinago
children.description: The enkelbeckasin — a widespread but declining breeder in Swedish bogs, marshes, and wet meadows. Its bill, one of the longest relative to body size of any bird, is sensitive at the tip, allowing it to detect prey by touch deep in soft mud. The spring drumming display — a distinctive bleating produced by vibrating outer tail feathers during a swooping dive — is heard on breeding grounds throughout Sweden from April to July. Winters in small numbers in southwestern Sweden; most migrate to west Africa.

children.name: Scolopax
children.description: Woodcocks — the most terrestrial of the sandpipers, inhabiting damp woodland and forest rather than open wetlands. The Eurasian woodcock (morkulla) is one of the most enigmatic birds in Sweden: its cryptic dead-leaf plumage makes it essentially invisible on the forest floor, and it is almost exclusively nocturnal. The one exception is the spectacular 'roding' display flight performed by males at dusk and dawn throughout spring: the male follows a regular circuit over the woodland canopy, uttering a series of deep grunting 'orr-orr-orr' sounds alternating with a high squeaky 'twick' — one of the most atmospheric and eagerly anticipated sounds in a Swedish spring evening. Woodcocks have eyes positioned high on the sides of their head, giving 360-degree vision — a complete field of view without turning their head.

children.name: Scolopax rusticola
children.description: The morkulla — a secretive woodland wader of damp mixed forest throughout Sweden. Almost never seen on the ground in daylight, relying on perfect camouflage to remain invisible among leaf litter. The evening roding display flight — a regular patrol over the treetops with its unique grunting and squeaking calls — is one of Sweden's most anticipated spring wildlife experiences. Eyes set high and to the rear of the head provide complete 360-degree vision for predator detection while the bill probes vertically into soil.

children.name: Tringa
children.description: Shanks, tattlers, and their allies — medium-sized, long-legged waders of wetland edges and mudflats that are among the most familiar waders in Sweden. The group contains some of Sweden's most abundant breeding waders (redshank, wood sandpiper) as well as common passage migrants and occasional winter visitors. Tringa sandpipers are characteristically nervous and alert birds — the redshank in particular functions as a sentinel species in Swedish wetlands, its loud, piping alarm calls instantly alerting every bird in the area to potential predators. Many Tringa species breed in Scandinavia and pass through Sweden in large numbers during autumn migration, when estuary mudflats and lake shores can be covered in feeding flocks.

children.name: Tringa totanus
children.description: The rödbena — a loud, nervous sentinel of Swedish coastal meadows, marshes, and lakeshores. Its bright orange-red bill base and legs, and distinctive white trailing edge to the wing in flight, make it unmistakeable. Breeds in wet grasslands and coastal meadows throughout Sweden; a declining species due to drainage. Its penetrating, yelping alarm calls are so effective that virtually every other wader in the vicinity responds instantly — earning it the nickname 'the warden of the marshes'.

children.name: Tringa nebularia
children.description: The gluttsnäppa — a large, elegant wader with a slightly upturned grey-green bill and loud, ringing three-note call: 'tew-tew-tew'. Breeds in open boggy forests in northern Sweden and Lapland; a common passage migrant through southern Sweden in spring and particularly autumn. Feeding birds wade actively in shallow water, sometimes chasing fish with surprising speed and agility. Its call — one of the most characteristic sounds of a northern Swedish bog in June — carries far across open moorland.

children.name: Tringa glareola
children.description: The grönbena — the most abundant and widespread Tringa wader on passage through Sweden. Its spotted brown upperparts, prominent white supercilium, and high-pitched 'chiff-if-if' call make it easy to identify. Breeds in wet forest clearings and boggy terrain throughout northern and central Sweden. In autumn, large flocks gather at Swedish freshwater wetlands during southward migration, making it one of the most numerous waders at inland Stockholm-area sites such as Kvarnsjön and Tröstnäs.

children.name: Actitis
children.description: Common sandpiper — a small, distinctive wader defined by its habit of constantly bobbing the rear of its body up and down as it walks. The common sandpiper (drillsnäppa) is one of the most widespread and familiar waders in Sweden, found on the banks and shores of virtually every lake, river, and stream from the southernmost lowlands to the Lapland mountains. It is characterised by a stiff-winged, low flight interspersed with glides on bowed wings just above the water surface, and by its incessant, high-pitched piping call. Pairs nest close to waterside on gravel banks, between rocks, or in vegetation; both parents share incubation and care of the downy chicks, which can run and feed themselves from hatching. The common sandpiper is one of the last migrants to arrive in Sweden each spring and one of the earliest to depart southward in summer.

children.name: Actitis hypoleucos
children.description: The drillsnäppa — Sweden's most widespread small wader, found bobbing along the banks of virtually every waterway in the country. Its constant tail-bobbing, stiff bowed-wing flight, and shrill 'twee-wee-wee' piping call are among the most familiar sights and sounds of a Swedish lakeside summer. Arrives in Sweden in late April from African wintering grounds. Chicks are precocial and follow parents along the water's edge from their first day. Can swim and dive when threatened.

children.name: Calidris
children.description: Stints and sandpipers — the most diverse genus of small waders, encompassing the majority of the world's diminutive shorebirds. In Sweden, Calidris species are primarily known as passage migrants and winter visitors to the coasts, with a few species breeding in Lapland. The dunlin (kärrsnäppa) is the most abundant small wader on the Swedish coast outside the breeding season; in autumn it forms huge, densely packed flocks — sometimes tens of thousands of birds — that wheel and turn over tidal flats in perfectly synchronised murmurations. The knot is famous for its massive staging concentrations at a handful of key sites along the Swedish coast, where flocks feeding on tidal mudflats can number in the thousands. These concentrations make Calidris waders among the most impressive wildlife spectacles in Sweden.

children.name: Calidris alpina
children.description: The kärrsnäppa — the most abundant small wader on the Swedish coast, present in huge numbers during spring and autumn migration and in smaller numbers throughout winter. In summer plumage its distinctive black belly patch and rufous back make it striking; in winter it is plain grey above and white below. Breeding populations in Lapland and Scandinavian mountain bogs are small but significant. Autumn flocks at Swedish estuaries — particularly along the Kattegat coast — can reach many thousands of birds, performing spectacular aerial manoeuvres over the tidal flats.

children.name: Calidris canutus
children.description: The kustsnäppa — a medium-sized wader famous for its enormous staging concentrations on Swedish and European coasts during migration. Breeds on high Arctic tundra; Swedish coasts serve as critical refuelling stops between breeding grounds in Canada/Greenland/Siberia and wintering areas in West Africa. In spring plumage the rich brick-red underparts are stunning; in winter birds are grey and white. The size of knot flocks using the Wadden Sea and Swedish west coast has declined dramatically in recent decades — a major conservation concern.
